h1Title: Achalasia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Achalasia is a primary esophageal motility disorder in which the lower esophageal sphincter fails to relax during swallowing and peristalsis is absent throughout the esophageal body. Affecting roughly 1 in 100,000 people per year globally, it is the most common primary motility disorder of the esophagus. The hallmark symptom is progressive dysphagia — difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids — accompanied by regurgitation of undigested food, chest pain, and weight loss. Without treatment, the esophagus dilates progressively, aspiration pneumonia may develop, and quality of life deteriorates substantially. Effective treatments including pneumatic dilation, Heller myotomy, and per-oral endoscopic myotomy (POEM) achieve long-term symptom control in over 80% of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence approximately 1 per 100,000 per year; estimated prevalence 10 per 100,000 in Western populations
keyStats.demographics: Affects men and women equally; rare before age 15, with peak incidence in the 30-60 age range and a second smaller peak after age 70
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis approximately 53 years; bimodal distribution with cases in both younger adults and the elderly
keyStats.globalCases: Roughly 10,000 new cases diagnosed per year in the United States; estimated 5-8 per 100,000 in Europe (European Gastroenterology Federation data)

definition: Achalasia (ICD-10: K22.0), also termed achalasia of the cardia or cardiospasm, is a primary esophageal neuromuscular disorder defined by absent or disordered peristalsis in the esophageal body combined with failure of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) to relax in response to swallowing. The fundamental lesion is selective destruction of inhibitory neurons (nitric oxide and vasoactive intestinal peptide-secreting) in the myenteric plexus of the LES and distal esophagus. The resulting unopposed excitatory tone keeps the LES tonically contracted. Stasis of food and saliva proximal to the obstructed cardia leads to progressive esophageal dilatation. Achalasia is classified by manometric sub-type using the Chicago Classification v4.0: Type I (aperistalsis with minimal pressurization), Type II (pan-esophageal pressurization in over 20% of swallows — best prognosis with treatment), and Type III (spastic contractions — premature or simultaneous, lowest treatment success rates). Secondary achalasia (pseudoachalasia) caused by infiltrating carcinoma, Chagas disease, or amyloid can mimic primary achalasia and must always be excluded before labelling the condition primary. The condition is managed by gastroenterologists, esophageal surgeons, and advanced endoscopists.

typesClassification.description: Complete absence of peristalsis with low integrated relaxation pressure (IRP) at the LES. Minimal esophageal pressurization. Represents approximately 20-35% of cases. Responds well to both pneumatic dilation and myotomy.

Absent peristalsis plus pan-esophageal pressurization during more than 20% of swallows. The most common subtype (about 50-60% of cases) and the one with the best response to any therapy; over 90% response to pneumatic dilation or POEM.

Preserved fragments of distal contractility occurring prematurely or simultaneously, producing spastic physiology. About 10-15% of cases. Predominantly chest-pain dominant. POEM may be preferred as it allows a longer myotomy tailored to the spastic segment.

Manometric and radiographic picture identical to primary achalasia but caused by an identifiable underlying condition: infiltrative gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) cancer, paraneoplastic syndrome, Chagas disease, amyloidosis, or post-fundoplication obstruction. Must be excluded before treatment, especially in patients over 60 with rapid onset or marked weight loss.

primarySymptoms: Progressive dysphagia for both solids and liquids, distinguishing achalasia from mechanical obstruction (where solids are affected first); present in over 90% of patients and typically worsens over months to years.
Regurgitation of undigested, non-acidic food and saliva hours after eating — often nocturnal, depositing material on the pillow; occurs in 75-90% of patients and poses aspiration risk.
Chest pain or pressure occurring during or between meals, often described as squeezing or heaviness, reported by 40-60% of patients and more prominent in Type III spastic achalasia.
Significant unintentional weight loss averaging 5-10 kg over the course of illness due to reduced oral intake from fear of eating and esophageal stasis.

Heartburn-like symptoms in roughly 30% of patients — caused by esophageal fermentation of retained food rather than gastric acid reflux, frequently leading to misdiagnosis as gastroesophageal reflux disease.
Choking, gagging, or coughing during meals as stasis-level regurgitation reaches the pharynx; particularly prominent when lying flat or bending over.
Nocturnal cough and recurrent aspiration pneumonia from regurgitated esophageal contents entering the airways during sleep.
Odynophagia (painful swallowing) reported in 30-40% of patients, typically midsternal, more common in spastic subtypes.
Slow or prolonged eating with the need to wash food down with large volumes of liquid — a compensatory strategy patients develop before seeking evaluation.
earlyWarningSigns: Paradoxical dysphagia: difficulty swallowing liquids as much as solids from the onset, which points away from a structural lesion and toward a motility cause.
Regurgitation of bland, non-sour, undigested food — the absence of acid taste distinguishes it from reflux and should prompt esophageal investigation.
Heartburn unresponsive after 4-8 weeks of proton-pump inhibitor therapy in a patient with swallowing symptoms, suggesting an alternative diagnosis.
Recurrent unexplained episodes of coughing, choking, or aspiration pneumonia without identified bronchopulmonary cause.
emergencySigns: Aspiration pneumonia with fever, productive cough, and infiltrates on chest imaging — requires urgent antibiotics and cessation of oral intake pending esophageal decompression.
Complete dysphagia for liquids with inability to swallow saliva — acute food bolus impaction or near-complete LES obstruction requiring emergency endoscopy.
Esophageal perforation after dilation — sudden severe chest pain, subcutaneous emphysema, fever, and pneumomediastinum on imaging; requires emergency surgical or endoscopic repair.
Massive aspiration with hypoxic respiratory failure — requires airway protection and intensive care admission.

causes.cause: Autoimmune destruction of myenteric inhibitory neurons
causes.description: The prevailing hypothesis is that an unknown environmental trigger (possibly viral — herpes simplex, measles, or varicella-zoster) initiates an autoimmune response in genetically susceptible individuals. Lymphocytic infiltration of the myenteric plexus selectively destroys nitric oxide synthase-expressing inhibitory interneurons, eliminating the signal for LES relaxation and coordinated peristalsis.

In Latin America, T. cruzi infection destroys the entire myenteric plexus, producing a clinical and manometric picture indistinguishable from primary achalasia. This is the most common identifiable cause worldwide and accounts for a substantial proportion of cases in Brazil, Argentina, and surrounding countries. T. cruzi serology is mandatory in patients from endemic areas.

Familial clustering occurs in roughly 1% of cases, with several case series describing autosomal-dominant and autosomal-recessive patterns. Mutations in genes regulating neural development and autonomic function have been implicated, though no single causative gene has been identified. Allgrove syndrome (triple-A syndrome) includes achalasia alongside adrenal insufficiency and alacrima and is caused by AAAS gene mutations.

Adenocarcinoma or squamous-cell carcinoma at the GEJ can mimic primary achalasia by physically obstructing the cardia and, in some cases, by producing a paraneoplastic anti-Hu neuropathy. Should always be excluded by endoscopy, endoscopic ultrasound, and cross-sectional imaging, particularly in patients over 60 with rapid onset of dysphagia and weight loss.

Amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, eosinophilic esophagitis with LES involvement, post-fundoplication obstruction, and paraneoplastic syndromes (anti-Hu, anti-ANNA-1 antibodies with lung or gastric cancer) can all produce secondary achalasia-like manometric patterns. Treatment of the underlying cause may partially reverse symptoms.

riskFactors.description: Primary achalasia has a bimodal age distribution peaking between 30-60 years and again after 70. The disease is rare in children and adolescents, representing fewer than 5% of cases.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or origin from Chagas-endemic Latin America
riskFactors.description: In T. cruzi-endemic areas of South America, Chagas cardiomyopathy and esophageal Chagas disease (megaesophagus) represent major causes of secondary achalasia, affecting an estimated 7-10 million people infected with T. cruzi globally.

Achalasia occurs at higher rates in individuals with other autoimmune conditions. Sjögren syndrome, systemic lupus, type 1 diabetes, and thyroid autoimmunity cluster with achalasia in cohort analyses, consistent with the autoimmune neuronal destruction hypothesis.

This rare autosomal-recessive syndrome caused by AAAS mutations presents with achalasia, adrenal insufficiency, and alacrima (absent tears). Achalasia in a child or teenager should trigger screening for this syndrome.

Epidemiological clustering of achalasia cases and the presence of herpes simplex virus, measles, and varicella-zoster antigens in resected LES specimens suggests viral triggering of the autoimmune process, though causality is unproven.
Achalasia occurs at a substantially elevated rate in individuals with trisomy 21 compared with the general population, suggesting a genetic contribution to esophageal neuromuscular development.

affectedDemographics: Equal sex distribution: unlike many GI disorders, achalasia affects men and women at nearly identical rates across all large registry studies.
Peak incidence in the fourth through sixth decades in primary achalasia; a secondary peak after age 70 warrants particular attention to exclude pseudoachalasia from GEJ cancer.
Children with achalasia represent fewer than 5% of all cases; familial forms and Allgrove syndrome should be suspected.
Individuals of Latin American origin: secondary (Chagasic) achalasia is highly prevalent; T. cruzi seroprevalence in migrants from endemic regions should guide evaluation.
Equal racial distribution: no consistent racial or ethnic predilection has been documented for primary achalasia in population-based studies.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis requires integrating clinical history with objective tests. The characteristic history — progressive dysphagia for solids and liquids, regurgitation of bland undigested food, heartburn unresponsive to PPIs, and weight loss — should prompt esophageal evaluation before empiric anti-reflux therapy is prolonged. Upper endoscopy is performed first to exclude pseudoachalasia from GEJ carcinoma; endoscopic findings of retained saliva or food, a dilated esophagus, and a puckered cardia that gives way to gentle scope pressure are supportive but not diagnostic. High-resolution esophageal manometry (HRM) with Clouse plot analysis is the gold standard: it quantifies LES resting pressure, IRP (integrated relaxation pressure), and the pattern of esophageal body pressurization, enabling Chicago Classification sub-typing that guides treatment choice. A barium esophagram showing a dilated esophagus tapering to a smooth bird-beak narrowing at the cardia is highly characteristic and also helps grade esophageal dilatation. In patients from Chagas-endemic areas, T. cruzi serology is mandatory. Where standard workup is equivocal, per-oral endoscopic functional lumen imaging probe (EndoFlip) provides intraoperative or endoscopic measurement of distensibility at the GEJ. CT chest or endoscopic ultrasound is added when pseudoachalasia is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard test to measure LES relaxation pressure and esophageal body peristalsis; enables Chicago Classification sub-typing
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin, flexible pressure-sensing catheter is passed through the nose into the stomach. You swallow 10 mL of water ten times over about 20 minutes while the catheter records pressures. Most patients tolerate it with mild discomfort. Results available within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes malignancy or structural causes of obstruction at the gastroesophageal junction before labeling the condition primary achalasia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sedated or unsedated scope procedure lasting 10-15 minutes. A camera on a flexible tube is passed down the throat. Retained food or saliva is washed away. The cardia is inspected for tumors or strictures. Recovery 30-60 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates the bird-beak narrowing at the cardia, measures esophageal diameter and emptying, and grades dilatation for surgical planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Drink 100-200 mL of barium liquid while X-rays are taken at 1 and 5 minutes. The radiologist measures the column height of retained barium. No preparation needed. Total time 15-20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates the mediastinum and GEJ for infiltrating tumor, lymphadenopathy, or other mass causing pseudoachalasia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-minute scan with IV contrast via an arm vein. No prior preparation beyond fasting. Report available within hours to days depending on setting.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses wall layers and surrounding lymph nodes when CT is equivocal and pseudoachalasia remains a concern
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Similar to standard endoscopy but the scope tip has an ultrasound transducer. Done under sedation, 20-30 minutes. Can biopsy suspicious nodes or wall thickening at the same time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures GEJ distensibility index; useful when manometry is equivocal or to guide the extent of myotomy intraoperatively
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A balloon catheter placed through the endoscope distends the cardia with saline while impedance planimetry measures the cross-sectional area. Done under sedation during endoscopy; adds minimal time.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of achalasia is aimed at mechanical disruption or pharmacological relaxation of the tonically contracted LES to restore gravity-assisted and bolus-pressure-driven esophageal emptying. No treatment restores normal peristalsis. Current ACG and AGA guidelines recommend pneumatic dilation (PD), laparoscopic Heller myotomy (LHM) with partial fundoplication, or per-oral endoscopic myotomy (POEM) as first-line endoscopic or surgical therapies for fit patients. The 2022 ACG Clinical Practice Guideline on Esophageal Motility Disorders provides a treatment algorithm stratified by Chicago Classification sub-type. For Type II achalasia — the most common and most treatment-responsive sub-type — all three modalities achieve symptom relief in over 85% of patients. POEM is preferred for Type III achalasia because it permits myotomy extending well proximal into the spastic esophageal segment, beyond what standard LHM allows. Pneumatic dilation requires a series of 30-35-40 mm balloons over multiple sessions, with a 3-5% perforation risk per dilation and approximately 25-35% recurrence requiring repeat dilation within five years. Laparoscopic Heller myotomy with Dor or Toupet fundoplication achieves 80-90% good-to-excellent outcomes at 5 years but carries a 5-10% risk of post-procedure GERD; POEM produces equivalent symptom relief but a higher rate of reflux (30-40% on pH-metry, though many are asymptomatic). Botulinum toxin injection into the LES is reserved for elderly or high-risk patients who cannot tolerate procedural therapies — it provides 3-6 months of symptom relief but requires repeated injections and does not prevent disease progression.

medicalTreatments.name: Pneumatic dilation (PD) — 30, 35, 40 mm graded balloons
medicalTreatments.description: Fluoroscopically or endoscopically guided balloon inflated at the LES to disrupt the circular muscle fibers. Graded protocol using sequentially larger balloons across 3-4 sessions 3 months apart is the standard approach, with the 40 mm balloon reserved for recurrences.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 85-90% initial symptom response; 5-year sustained remission in 60-70%; 3-5% risk of esophageal perforation per session

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin injection (Botox 80-100 U in 4 quadrants at LES)
medicalTreatments.description: Endoscopic injection of botulinum toxin into the LES to paralyze excitatory acetylcholine-releasing neurons, temporarily lowering LES pressure. Primarily for patients too frail or unwilling to undergo dilation or surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Initial response 70-85%, but median duration of benefit only 6-18 months; 50% of patients require repeat injection within 6-12 months

medicalTreatments.name: Calcium channel blockers (nifedipine 10-20 mg sublingual before meals) or long-acting nitrates (isosorbide dinitrate 5 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Smooth-muscle relaxants that reduce LES pressure by 30-40%. Used only as bridging therapy before definitive intervention or in patients who refuse all procedures. Headache and hypotension limit tolerability.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest symptom improvement in 50-70%; no data on structural progression prevention; symptom benefit is inferior to all procedural therapies

medicalTreatments.name: Per-oral endoscopic myotomy (POEM)
medicalTreatments.description: Endoscopic tunnel created in the submucosal space; myotomy of circular muscle fibers performed from 2-3 cm above the GEJ to 1-3 cm below. Allows precise tailoring of myotomy length, particularly for Type III achalasia requiring extended proximal myotomy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical success (Eckardt score ≤3) in 90-95% at 1 year and 85-90% at 3 years; gastroesophageal reflux develops in 30-40% on 24-hour pH monitoring

medicalTreatments.name: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy (LHM) with partial fundoplication
medicalTreatments.description: Laparoscopic division of the LES circular and longitudinal muscle fibers (5-6 cm esophageal, 1.5-2 cm gastric extent) combined with Dor (anterior) or Toupet (posterior) 180-270-degree partial fundoplication to limit post-myotomy reflux.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Good-to-excellent outcomes in 80-90% at 5 years; GERD in 5-10% with fundoplication; mean length of stay 1-2 days

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy (LHM) with partial fundoplication
surgicalOptions.description: Standard-of-care surgical option since the 1990s. Five laparoscopic ports; myotomy carried 5-6 cm onto the esophagus and 1.5-2 cm onto the stomach; Dor or Toupet wrap controls reflux. Total operative time 60-120 minutes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 80-90% symptom control at 5 years; conversion to open surgery less than 2% in experienced centers

surgicalOptions.name: Esophagectomy (salvage for end-stage achalasia)
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for patients with a massively dilated, tortuous (sigmoid) esophagus who have failed multiple prior interventions. Minimally invasive esophagectomy with gastric pull-up or colonic interposition is performed at high-volume centers.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Significant symptom relief in 80-90% of patients with end-stage disease; 30-day mortality less than 2% at experienced centers; 5-year survival excellent when no malignancy coexists

alternativeTreatments.name: Endoscopic balloon dilation as maintenance therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Repeat pneumatic dilation sessions at 6-12 month intervals have been shown in the European Achalasia Trial to achieve outcomes equivalent to laparoscopic Heller myotomy over 5 years in a majority of patients, making it a viable long-term management strategy rather than a bridge to surgery.

whySeeSpecialist: A gastroenterologist or esophageal surgeon with high-resolution manometry expertise is required to sub-type achalasia correctly and select appropriate therapy. POEM and Heller myotomy outcomes are strongly center-volume dependent — hospitals performing more than 20 myotomies per year show significantly lower complication rates and higher long-term success. Referral to a tertiary esophageal center is warranted when initial therapy fails or relapse occurs within 12 months.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a gastroenterologist who interprets high-resolution manometry using the Chicago Classification and a surgeon or advanced endoscopist credentialed in POEM. Ask how many myotomies or dilations the center performs per year. An esophageal center with combined gastroenterology, thoracic surgery, and radiology expertise gives the best outcomes.
hospitalCriteria: High-resolution esophageal manometry (HRM) with Chicago Classification v4.0 reporting capability
Fluoroscopy or endoscopy unit for pneumatic dilation
Advanced endoscopy unit capable of performing POEM (submucosal tunneling)
Laparoscopic surgical program with experience in Heller myotomy and fundoplication
24-hour esophageal pH-impedance monitoring for post-treatment reflux assessment
keyFacilities: Academic gastroenterology units with HRM laboratories
Advanced endoscopy centers with POEM programs
Minimally invasive thoracic and upper GI surgery programs
Nutrition support teams for managing weight loss in severe or delayed-presentation achalasia
preventionStrategies: No modifiable primary prevention strategy exists for primary achalasia because the autoimmune etiology is not preventable; early referral for dysphagia evaluation prevents years of misdiagnosis as GERD.
In Chagas-endemic areas, use insecticide-treated materials and avoid sleeping in thatched housing to reduce T. cruzi exposure, preventing Chagasic megaesophagus.
Screen first-degree relatives of individuals with familial achalasia or Allgrove syndrome for early signs of dysphagia.
Avoid prolonged empirical PPI therapy in patients with dysphagia and regurgitation without confirmed reflux — delayed diagnosis leads to esophageal dilatation that worsens treatment outcomes.
lifestyleModifications: Eat slowly, chew food thoroughly, and sit upright during and for at least 30 minutes after every meal to assist gravity-driven esophageal emptying.
Elevate the head of the bed by 15-20 cm to reduce nocturnal regurgitation and aspiration risk.
Avoid eating within 2-3 hours of bedtime; keep a log of symptom-provoking foods and adjust the diet accordingly.
Drink water or warm liquids throughout meals to help flush boluses through the poorly relaxing LES.
Consume soft, well-cooked, or blended foods during symptomatic periods; avoid sticky textures such as bread, rice, and steak that are particularly prone to stasis.
After POEM or Heller myotomy, follow a liquid-to-soft food progression over 2-4 weeks per the surgical team's guidance.
Seek prompt medical attention for cough, fever, or worsening dysphagia — these may indicate aspiration pneumonia or treatment failure requiring re-evaluation.

recommended: Warm or room-temperature liquids and soups that pass more easily through the tonically contracted LES than cold beverages
Well-cooked, soft vegetables, fish, and tender poultry as protein sources less prone to esophageal impaction
Smoothies and high-calorie liquid nutritional supplements during periods of significant weight loss
Small, frequent meals (4-6 per day) rather than 2-3 large meals to reduce the volume requiring transit at each sitting
avoid: Bread, white rice, pasta, and other starchy, sticky textures that clump and impede transit
Tough red meat and high-fiber raw vegetables that require vigorous chewing and are prone to bolus obstruction
Cold carbonated beverages that may trigger spasm in Type III achalasia
Large meals within 2-3 hours of bed that increase nocturnal regurgitation and aspiration risk

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise is safe and encouraged to maintain body weight and nutritional status. Recumbency should be avoided for at least 60 minutes after exercise-associated eating. Core-strengthening exercises in supine positions should be timed carefully to avoid regurgitation. Patients with aspiration pneumonia should defer aerobic exercise until clinical resolution.
dailyManagement: Take any prescribed smooth-muscle relaxant (nifedipine or nitrate) 30 minutes before meals if still awaiting definitive therapy.
Elevate the head of the bed every night; do not sleep flat.
Record dysphagia frequency and severity using the Eckardt score (0-12 scale) at each clinic visit; an Eckardt score above 3 indicates treatment failure.
After POEM or Heller myotomy, attend annual endoscopy and pH-impedance monitoring to screen for post-procedural GERD.
Report new or worsening dysphagia promptly — may indicate disease progression, post-procedural stricture, or reflux esophagitis.
prognosis: With treatment, the majority of patients with achalasia achieve sustained symptom relief and a near-normal quality of life. Type II achalasia responds best — over 90% of patients achieve clinical remission following POEM, Heller myotomy, or graded pneumatic dilation. Type I and Type III subtypes have slightly lower long-term success rates (75-85% and 65-80% at 5 years respectively). Relapse occurs in 20-30% of patients over 10 years regardless of modality and often responds to repeat treatment. The principal long-term risk in treated achalasia is post-procedure gastroesophageal reflux, which — if unmanaged — can progress to erosive esophagitis, Barrett esophagus, and adenocarcinoma. Untreated or inadequately treated achalasia carries a small but real risk of esophageal squamous-cell carcinoma (relative risk approximately 16-28 times the general population over decades of stasis). Surveillance endoscopy every 3-5 years after 15 years of disease is recommended in several guidelines.
recoveryTimeline: Symptom improvement begins within days of successful pneumatic dilation or myotomy. Soft diet restrictions are lifted within 2-4 weeks. Return to full activity is expected within 2-6 weeks after laparoscopic Heller myotomy or POEM. Post-POEM, 24-hour pH testing is typically performed at 3-6 months to quantify reflux and guide PPI therapy.
complications: Post-procedural gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD): occurs in 30-40% after POEM and 5-10% after Heller myotomy with fundoplication; monitor with pH-impedance testing and treat with PPIs or repeat fundoplication if severe.
Esophageal perforation: 3-5% risk per pneumatic dilation session; presents as chest pain, subcutaneous emphysema, and pneumomediastinum on CT; requires immediate endoscopic or surgical repair.
Aspiration pneumonia: results from nocturnal regurgitation of retained esophageal contents; treat with antibiotics and expedite definitive therapy.
Sigmoid (end-stage) esophagus: massive dilatation and tortuosity developing after decades of inadequate treatment, requiring esophagectomy in refractory cases.
Esophageal squamous-cell carcinoma: long-term relative risk 16-28-fold above baseline; endoscopic surveillance every 3-5 years after 15 years of disease duration is advised by ACG guidelines.
Barrett esophagus and esophageal adenocarcinoma: complication of post-procedural GERD; annual endoscopy for confirmed Barrett; PPIs reduce progression risk.

supportResources.name: International Foundation for Gastrointestinal Disorders (IFFGD)
supportResources.description: Patient information, provider finder, and support network for esophageal and GI motility disorders including achalasia.

supportResources.name: ACG Patient Center — Achalasia
supportResources.description: American College of Gastroenterology's patient-facing achalasia overview with treatment decision guidance.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GERD causes heartburn and regurgitation of sour, acidic material from the stomach, relieved by PPIs and triggered by fatty foods. Achalasia causes regurgitation of bland, non-acidic, undigested food hours after eating, is not relieved by PPIs, and manometry shows absent peristalsis and elevated LES pressure rather than a hypotensive sphincter.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peptic ulcer disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peptic ulcer produces epigastric pain related to meals (improved or worsened depending on site), without dysphagia or regurgitation. Upper endoscopy shows mucosal ulceration. Achalasia produces dysphagia and regurgitation with endoscopic finding of retained esophageal contents and a puckered cardia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pyloric stenosis obstructs the gastric outlet, causing projectile vomiting of partly digested acidic stomach contents shortly after eating. Achalasia obstructs the esophageal inlet, causing regurgitation of undigested, non-acidic food. Barium study and endoscopy clearly localize the level of obstruction to the pylorus versus the cardia.

relatedConditions.name: Gastroesophageal reflux disease
relatedConditions.relevance: The main condition achalasia is misdiagnosed as; post-treatment achalasia creates genuine GERD that requires long-term acid suppression monitoring.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic pancreatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another upper GI motility-adjacent gastroenterology condition managed in the same specialty; both produce significant nutritional consequences.

relatedConditions.name: Alcoholic liver disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares gastroenterology specialty management and may coexist in patients with multi-organ GI disease.

faqs.question: What is achalasia of the cardia?
faqs.answer: Achalasia of the cardia is a neuromuscular disorder of the esophagus in which the lower esophageal sphincter fails to relax when swallowing and peristalsis is absent. Food and liquid accumulate above the obstruction, causing dysphagia, regurgitation, and weight loss. It is caused by destruction of inhibitory nerves in the esophageal wall and is treated with dilation, POEM, or surgical myotomy.

faqs.question: What are the first symptoms of achalasia?
faqs.answer: The first symptoms are usually progressive difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids (dysphagia) and regurgitation of bland, undigested food that does not taste sour. Many patients also report heartburn that does not improve with antacids. Symptoms typically worsen slowly over months to years before a diagnosis is made.

faqs.question: Is achalasia curable?
faqs.answer: No therapy restores normal peristalsis, but treatment effectively relieves obstruction. POEM, Heller myotomy, and graded pneumatic dilation each achieve long-term symptom remission in 80-90% of patients. Relapse occurs in 20-30% within 10 years and usually responds to repeat treatment. Regular follow-up is necessary to manage post-treatment reflux.

faqs.question: What is the difference between achalasia and GERD?
faqs.answer: GERD causes acidic reflux relieved by proton-pump inhibitors. Achalasia causes regurgitation of bland, undigested food hours after eating that is not relieved by PPIs. Manometry shows absent esophageal peristalsis and a hypertensive non-relaxing sphincter in achalasia, contrasting with the hypotensive or normal sphincter found in GERD.

faqs.question: How is achalasia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires upper endoscopy (to exclude cancer), high-resolution esophageal manometry (the gold standard, showing absent peristalsis and elevated integrated relaxation pressure), and a barium swallow (showing the classic bird-beak tapering at the cardia). Manometry also sub-types achalasia as Type I, II, or III, which guides treatment choice.

faqs.question: What is POEM for achalasia?
faqs.answer: POEM (per-oral endoscopic myotomy) is an advanced endoscopic procedure where a tunnel is created under the esophageal lining and the muscle fibers keeping the sphincter closed are divided with a knife-tip electrocautery tool. It is done under general anesthesia and leaves no external incisions. Clinical success exceeds 90% at one year, and it is especially favored for Type III spastic achalasia.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for achalasia?
faqs.answer: POEM, laparoscopic Heller myotomy with partial fundoplication, and graded pneumatic dilation are all guideline-recommended first-line therapies with comparable long-term outcomes. POEM is preferred for Type III achalasia and may offer a slight symptom advantage, but carries a higher post-procedure reflux rate. Choice depends on patient anatomy, subtype, comorbidities, and center expertise.

faqs.question: Can achalasia cause cancer?
faqs.answer: Long-standing achalasia increases the risk of esophageal squamous-cell carcinoma 16-28 times above baseline, likely due to chronic mucosal irritation from food stasis and fermentation. Post-treatment GERD also raises the risk of Barrett esophagus and adenocarcinoma. Surveillance endoscopy every 3-5 years after 15 years of disease is recommended in ACG guidelines.

faqs.question: Can achalasia be treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pneumatic dilation, POEM, and botulinum toxin injection are non-surgical options. Graded pneumatic dilation achieves 5-year remission equivalent to surgery in controlled trials for Type II achalasia. Botulinum toxin is reserved for patients unable to tolerate more durable procedures, as its benefit lasts only 6-18 months.

faqs.question: What causes achalasia?
faqs.answer: Primary achalasia is caused by autoimmune destruction of inhibitory nerves in the myenteric plexus of the lower esophageal sphincter, possibly triggered by a viral infection in genetically susceptible individuals. Secondary achalasia is caused by Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi infection), infiltrating gastroesophageal junction cancer, amyloidosis, or paraneoplastic syndromes.

faqs.question: Does achalasia get worse over time if untreated?
faqs.answer: Yes. Without treatment the lower esophageal sphincter remains obstructed, the esophagus dilates progressively, and food stasis worsens. Years of untreated disease produce a massively dilated, tortuous sigmoid esophagus that may require esophagectomy. Aspiration pneumonia, malnutrition, and esophageal cancer risk all increase with disease duration.

faqs.question: Can achalasia cause weight loss?
faqs.answer: Significant unintentional weight loss averaging 5-10 kg is common in achalasia due to reduced oral intake from fear of eating, prolonged meals, and postprandial regurgitation. Nutritional assessment and dietitian input are important parts of care, particularly in patients who are markedly underweight at diagnosis or following treatment.

faqs.question: Is achalasia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Primary achalasia is not typically inherited, but familial cases account for about 1% and are described with autosomal-dominant and autosomal-recessive patterns. Allgrove (triple-A) syndrome — an autosomal-recessive AAAS gene mutation — includes achalasia as one of its three defining features and should be considered in any child or adolescent with achalasia.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after POEM for achalasia?
faqs.answer: Most patients start clear liquids the morning after POEM, progress to soft foods over 2 weeks, and return to full diet within 3-4 weeks. Hospital stay is typically 1-2 days. Return to work usually occurs within 1-2 weeks for desk-based roles. A 24-hour pH study at 3-6 months assesses for post-POEM reflux.

faqs.question: Does achalasia cause aspiration pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Regurgitation of retained esophageal contents during sleep can reach the airways and cause aspiration pneumonia, presenting with fever, cough, and lung infiltrates. Recurrent unexplained pneumonia, especially in the right lower lobe, should prompt esophageal investigation. Elevating the head of the bed and expediting definitive therapy reduce this risk.

symptomKeywords: unexplained weight loss with eating difficulty
metaDescription: Achalasia: esophageal motility disorder causing dysphagia and regurgitation. Symptoms, manometry diagnosis, POEM and Heller myotomy treatment reviewed.
